subject: Mathematics
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Week 1, Days 2–5: Math Stream

lesson_overview.overview: Math-stream students complete four task sheets across Days 2–5 of Week 1. Day 1 estimates are replaced with real data: students extract the budget from the client brief, compare design dimensions from cards, calculate the area of each option, and then for the chosen design calculate volume and estimate soil bags. By Day 5 they have a complete Week 1 measurement record.
lesson_overview.essential_question: What numbers do we need, and what can we calculate from them?
lesson_overview.big_idea: Math gives us a way to compare options and make decisions we can defend with evidence — not just gut feeling.

sequence.focus: Read the client brief for numbers: budget, dimensions of the space, timeline.
Record dimensions of three design options. Calculate and compare areas.
For the chosen design: calculate area, volume, and estimate soil bags needed.
Week 1 measurement record. Check calculations. Identify what to measure next.

lesson_overview.differentiation_note: Three support levels. Level A: formula printed on sheet, calculation steps pre-structured with blanks, number sense check ('does this seem right?'). Level B: formula reminder, open calculation space, comparison question. Level C: open problems, extend to cost estimation or comparison of all three designs.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Math-stream students are making real calculations that the project depends on. Their area and volume numbers will feed into materials orders and budget decisions.
learning_goals: Extract numerical data (budget, dimensions) from a functional document.
Calculate the area of a rectangle using A = L × W for three designs.
Calculate the volume of a rectangular prism using V = L × W × H for the chosen design.
Estimate the number of soil bags needed by converting volume to litres.
materials: Design cards A, B, C — with dimensions clearly labeled
Ruler — for Day 3 if measuring physical cards

timing.activity: Math-stream task time. Teacher circulates. Calculator available throughout.

teacher_notes: Day 2: model how to find numbers in a text document — circle, box, or highlight before recording.
Day 3: show A = L × W on the board before students attempt it. Work through Design A together first.
Day 4: volume is new for Math 8 students. Walk through V = L × W × H step by step before independent work.
Day 5: the measurement record is a reference document — accuracy matters. Students check their own work.
Workplace Math 10 students can extend Day 4 to include cost per bag × number of bags = soil cost.

days.day_label: Day 2 — Numbers in the Brief (Math Stream)
days.carryover_note: Day 1 step-estimates are a reference. Today we extract real numbers from the client brief.

task_sheet.title: Day 2 — Find the Numbers in the Brief
instructions: Your teacher will read the client brief aloud.
As you listen, look for numbers: budget, size limits, and timeline.
Circle or underline every number you hear.

tasks.label: Part 1: Extract the numbers
tasks.prompt:
Read or listen to the client brief. Record every number you find.

Budget per planter: $___________________
Maximum space available: ___ m × ___ m (or ___ cm × ___ cm)
Number of planters needed: ___________________
Timeline (weeks): ___________________
Any other numbers I found: ___________________

render_hints.heading: Numbers from the Brief
render_hints.help: Numbers hide in sentences. Look for: $, cm, m, weeks, and counts like 'two' or 'three'.

tasks.label: Part 2: Budget breakdown thinking
tasks.prompt:
The total budget is $_____ per planter.

Before we calculate real costs, estimate how much each category might cost:

Wood / lumber: about $___
Screws and hardware: about $___
Soil: about $___
Plants or seeds: about $___

Total estimate: $___

Is this more or less than the budget? (circle)   MORE   LESS   ABOUT THE SAME

render_hints.help: These are guesses — real prices come later. The goal is to think about where the money goes.

tasks.label: Part 3: My Day 1 estimate vs. today
tasks.prompt:
On Day 1, I estimated the space was about _____ cm wide and _____ cm long.

Does the brief give us any size limits for the planter? Y / N
If yes, what are they? ___________________

Do my Day 1 estimates seem reasonable for that space? Y / N
Why or why not? ___________________

render_hints.help: Check your Day 1 worksheet. Compare your estimate to any size information in the brief.

embedded_supports: Level A: numbers are pre-circled in a printed copy of the brief
Formula reminder posted: A = L × W
success_criteria: I found the budget number in the brief and recorded it.
I estimated costs for at least three categories.
I compared my Day 1 estimate to what the brief tells us.

days.carryover_note: Design cards A, B, C must be available. Calculators needed.

task_sheet.title: Day 3 — Measure and Compare the Designs
instructions: Use the design cards to find the dimensions of each planter option.
Record and calculate. Show your work.

tasks.label: Part 1: Record dimensions
tasks.prompt:
Find the length (L), width (W), and height (H) for each design.

Design A: L = _____ cm   W = _____ cm   H = _____ cm
Design B: L = _____ cm   W = _____ cm   H = _____ cm
Design C: L = _____ cm   W = _____ cm   H = _____ cm

Which is the tallest? Design ___
Which is the longest? Design ___

render_hints.heading: Design Dimensions
render_hints.help: Read the numbers from the design card. Write the unit (cm) after every number.

tasks.label: Part 2: Calculate area
tasks.prompt:
Calculate the planting surface area (A = L × W) for each design.

Design A: A = _____ × _____ = __________ cm²
Design B: A = _____ × _____ = __________ cm²
Design C: A = _____ × _____ = __________ cm²

Largest area: Design ___ with __________ cm²
Smallest area: Design ___ with __________ cm²
Difference between largest and smallest: __________ cm²

render_hints.help: Area = Length × Width. Answer is always in cm² (square centimetres). Use your calculator.

tasks.label: Part 3: Best fit for the budget
tasks.prompt:
More area usually means more materials and higher cost.

Based on area alone, which design gives the client the most planting space? Design ___

But does a bigger design always mean better value? Circle: YES   NO   DEPENDS

Explain in one sentence: ___________________

render_hints.help: Think: a bigger planter costs more to fill with soil and more wood to build. Is it always worth it?

embedded_supports: Formula card: A = L × W (area = length × width)
Unit reminder: area is always cm² or m²
Level A: calculation steps pre-printed — fill in the dimension numbers only
success_criteria: I recorded all three dimensions (L, W, H) for each design.
I calculated the area of each design using A = L × W.
I identified which design has the most planting area.

days.day_label: Day 4 — Calculate for the Chosen Design (Math Stream)
days.carryover_note: The class has voted. Teacher confirms the chosen design at the start. Students use that design only.

task_sheet.title: Day 4 — Area, Volume, and Soil Estimate
instructions: The class chose Design ___. (Confirmed by teacher.)
Use those dimensions for all calculations today.
Show every step. Write units with every answer.

tasks.label: Part 1: Confirm the dimensions
tasks.prompt:
Chosen design: Design ___

L = __________ cm
W = __________ cm
H = __________ cm

Area (L × W) = __________ × __________ = __________ cm²

Does this match your Day 3 calculation? Y / N

render_hints.heading: Our Design — Key Numbers
render_hints.help: Copy from your Day 3 sheet or the design card. Check that you are using the right design.

tasks.label: Part 2: Calculate volume
tasks.prompt:
Volume tells us how much soil fits inside the planter.

Formula: V = L × W × H

V = __________ × __________ × __________ = __________ cm³

Convert to litres: __________ cm³ ÷ 1000 = __________ litres

Does this volume seem reasonable for a planter box? Y / N

render_hints.help: Formula: V = L × W × H. Then divide by 1000 to get litres. 1000 cm³ = 1 litre.

tasks.label: Part 3: Estimate soil bags
tasks.prompt:
One standard bag of potting soil holds about 20 litres.

Litres needed: __________ L
÷ 20 litres per bag = __________ bags
Round UP to: __________ bags

If one bag costs $8.50:
Total soil cost = __________ bags × $8.50 = $__________

Extension (WM10): What percent of the $250 budget does soil take up?
$__________ ÷ $250 × 100 = __________%

render_hints.help: Always round UP for bags — you cannot buy half a bag. WM10 extension: percent = (part ÷ whole) × 100.

embedded_supports: Formula card: V = L × W × H; 1 bag = 20 L; always round up
Level A: step-by-step calculation chain printed — fill in the numbers
success_criteria: I confirmed the area of the chosen design.
I calculated the volume using V = L × W × H.
I estimated how many soil bags we need and rounded up.

days.carryover_note: Students bring all math task sheets from Days 1–4. This is a review and record-keeping day.

task_sheet.title: Day 5 — Week 1 Measurement Record
instructions: Today you complete your Week 1 measurement record.
Use your task sheets from Days 2–4 to fill it in.
This record stays in your project folder — it is a reference for the whole project.

tasks.label: Part 1: Complete the measurement record
tasks.prompt:
Fill in everything we know about our chosen design.

Chosen design: Design ___

Length:  __________ cm
Width:   __________ cm
Height:  __________ cm

Area (L × W):           __________ cm²
Volume (L × W × H):    __________ cm³
Volume in litres:        __________ L
Soil bags needed:        __________ bags (rounded up)
Estimated soil cost:     $__________

render_hints.heading: Week 1 Measurement Record
render_hints.help: Copy from your Days 2–4 sheets. Check every unit. This record goes in your project folder.

tasks.label: Part 2: Check your calculations
tasks.prompt:
Check each answer by asking: does this make sense?

Area of __________ cm² — does this seem like a reasonable planter surface? Y / N
Volume of __________ cm³ — is this more or less than 50 000 cm³? (circle)  MORE   LESS
Soil bags: __________ — a standard planter usually needs 3–8 bags. Are we in range? Y / N

One number I want to recheck: ___________________

render_hints.help: Reasonableness check: typical planter dimensions 60–120 cm long, 30–60 cm wide, 20–40 cm tall.

tasks.label: Part 3: What do we still need to measure?
tasks.prompt:
We calculated from the design card. Next week we measure the REAL space.

List two measurements you want to take of the actual site next week:

1. ___________________
2. ___________________

What tool will you use? (circle)   ruler   tape measure   metre stick   steps

render_hints.help: Think: does the design fit the space? What do we need to verify with real measurements?

embedded_supports: Task sheets from Days 2–4 available as reference
Formula reference: A = L × W; V = L × W × H; 1000 cm³ = 1 L
Level A: record form is pre-labelled — write numbers only
success_criteria: My measurement record is complete with all eight fields filled in.
Every measurement has a unit (cm, cm², cm³, L, bags).
I can name two measurements I still need to take from the real space.

exit_ticket.title: Day 5 Exit Check — Math Stream
exit_ticket.prompt:
Without looking at your sheet — answer from memory:

Our planter is ___ cm long, ___ cm wide, and ___ cm tall.
Its area is about ___ cm².
We need about ___ bags of soil.
success_criteria: Student recalls all three dimensions (approximate is fine).
Student can estimate the area and soil bag count.
